The US and Iran continue to exchange fire in the Strait of Hormuz despite ongoing ceasefire negotiations. Iran has seized two cargo ships in the strait and stated that reopening it is 'impossible' if the US naval blockade continues.

The European Union has approved a €90 billion loan for Ukraine as the Druzhba oil pipeline resumes operations, ending months of deadlock. Ukraine restarted oil transit to Hungary and Slovakia after completing repairs to the pipeline, which had been damaged by Russian attacks.

China's economy is facing new pressures from the US-Iran war, with manufacturing hubs like Foshan experiencing reduced factory orders, increased costs, and job losses. While China weathered Trump's tariffs last year, the Middle East conflict is now taking a toll on its export-driven economy.
CIA analysis suggests Iran could withstand the US naval blockade for another four months, indicating limited US leverage in negotiations.

ProPublica investigates the surge in immigration scams under Trump’s mass-deportation campaign. The piece follows Jasmir Urbina, an asylum-seeker who paid nearly $10,000 to a fake ‘Catholic Charities’ attorney via WhatsApp and Zelle, only to discover she had been defrauded.

ai_systems.findings: Two new autonomous research systems push beyond static benchmarks. EvoMaster (arXiv 2604.17406) introduces a foundational "evolving agent framework" that iteratively refines hypotheses and accumulates knowledge across experimental cycles, achieving 75.8 % on MLE-Bench Lite and 53.3 % on FrontierScience—gains of +159 % to +316 % over OpenClaw. Complementing it, Ouroboros (agent-wars.com, 15 Mar) layers a generational meta-loop on Karpathy’s autoresearch codebase: each cycle rewrites its own methodology document (genome.md), tracks prediction-outcome divergence, and archives full lineage artifacts for auditability. Meanwhile, label-free prompt optimization matures: UPA (arXiv 2601.23273) uses tree-structured search with LLM pairwise comparisons to optimize prompts without ground-truth labels, and Self-Supervised Prompt Optimization (arXiv 2502.06855) cuts optimization cost to 1.1–5.6 % of supervised methods by replacing gold labels with output-comparison signals. Together, these advances [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Rep. Anna Paulina Luna’s House Oversight Task Force is actively investigating UAP transparency in 2026. After a September 2025 hearing with whistleblowers, the panel demanded 46 unreleased videos from the Pentagon’s AARO; Luna says she has personally seen “non-human origin” evidence in classified briefings and vows a public press conference once the material is declassified. The probe is framed as a national-security imperative, with subpoenas threatened if the Department of War continues to withhold records.

weather:
Based on the latest weather data for Edmonds, Washington today:

**Current Conditions:** Overcast with increasing clouds throughout the day, transitioning to times of clouds and sun. Current temperature is 53°F, feeling like 51°F.

**Today's Forecast:** High near 67°F with a low around 53°F tonight. Very low precipitation chance at only 2%. Winds will be calm, becoming north-northwest around 6 mph in the afternoon.

**Evening Outlook:** Mostly cloudy tonight with a low around 51°F. Winds will be north-northwest around 6 mph, becoming calm after midnight. No rain expected this evening.

local_news.findings: The Washington State Court of Appeals denied a motion to reconsider its March 16 dismissal of the Rimmer v. City of Edmonds case. This ends the four-year legal battle over a dogwood tree that began when Nathan Rimmer's permit was denied in May 2022. While the Appeals Court dismissed the case as "moot," Rimmer's attorneys plan to ask the Washington Supreme Court to provide clarity on whether property owners can recover damages when cities withhold permits to force unconstitutional conditions.

The Edmonds City Council postponed until next week its vote on confirming Stephanie Lucash as the new city administrator. Lucash, currently deputy city manager for Kenmore, would receive an annual base salary of $226,068 if confirmed. The council also advanced a measure to "unpause" the volunteer salary commission to review elected officials' compensation for the 2027-2028 biennium, with Mayor Rosen breaking a 3-3 tie to send the proposal to next week's consent agenda.

Edmonds Police are investigating the theft of a South County Fire Ford F-150 pickup truck that was stolen from the scene of a residential fire investigation on Friday. The vehicle was taken while firefighters were responding to an emergency call. Police have not released details about suspects or the circumstances of the theft.

A man was arrested for stalking after repeatedly contacting a woman in the 1100 block of 7th Avenue South. The incident was part of the Edmonds Police blotter covering April 20-26, 2026. Additionally, police responded to a boat license plate incident in the 300 block of Admiral Way, though details of this incident were not specified in the report.

career.notes: Most districts are using pool postings for 2026-2027 secondary positions rather than specific job postings. Snohomish School District lists the only specific salary range found. All districts within 30 miles of Edmonds are actively recruiting for English and History teaching positions with applications typically reviewed April-June and August. Candidates should apply to multiple district pools to maximize opportunities.

global_news.findings: The US and Iran continue to exchange fire in the Strait of Hormuz despite ongoing ceasefire negotiations. Iran has seized two cargo ships in the strait and stated that reopening it is 'impossible' if the US naval blockade continues. The US military reported intercepting Iranian attacks on three navy destroyers and conducting strikes on Iranian vessels. Meanwhile, a 14-point US proposal to end the war is under consideration by Iran, with Pakistani mediators facilitating discussions. President Trump has predicted the war will end 'quickly' if Iran agrees to the terms.

The European Union has approved a €90 billion loan for Ukraine as the Druzhba oil pipeline resumes operations, ending months of deadlock. Ukraine restarted oil transit to Hungary and Slovakia after completing repairs to the pipeline, which had been damaged by Russian attacks. This development follows the electoral defeat of Hungary's Viktor Orbán, whose previous veto had blocked the EU funding. The loan is described as 'a matter of life and death' for Kyiv, with two-thirds allocated for defense needs.

China's economy is facing new pressures from the US-Iran war, with manufacturing hubs like Foshan experiencing reduced factory orders, increased costs, and job losses. While China weathered Trump's tariffs last year, the Middle East conflict is now taking a toll on its export-driven economy. Workers in manufacturing centers report struggling to earn enough to send money home, as global supply chains continue to be disrupted by the ongoing conflict.

CIA analysis suggests Iran could withstand the US naval blockade for another four months, indicating limited US leverage in negotiations. Recent days have seen the largest flare-ups in fighting around the Strait of Hormuz since the ceasefire began, with the UAE reporting attacks from Iranian ballistic missiles and drones. The US Treasury has announced new sanctions against individuals and companies aiding Iran's military efforts, including entities in China and Hong Kong, as diplomatic efforts continue amid ongoing military clashes.

The Pentagon has released a new tranche of declassified UFO documents spanning decades, including reports from Apollo astronauts describing unexplained flashes of light and objects on the Moon. The 161 files contain military memos, civilian sightings, and footage from US operations in Iraq, Syria, and the UAE. President Trump directed the release, which includes accounts from astronauts like Buzz Aldrin and transcripts from Apollo missions describing 'particles sailing off into space' and unexplained light phenomena.

intellectual_journals.findings: Flora Champy revisits Marcel Proust’s 1905 essay 'On Reading' to defend literature as a portal to personal freedom rather than a moral improvement device. Proust rejects John Ruskin’s Victorian claim that books must "pay" by making readers better people; instead, he argues that reading is a unique, disinterested communication that lets us commune with the finest minds outside the distortions of social life. Champy shows how Proust’s view—that literature preserves past sensations and makes time graspable without abolishing it—anticipates his later novel In Search of Lost Time and offers a count [TRUNCATED]

Christopher de Bellaigue surveys post-war Iran in 'Iran’s New Winter'. He traces the 1953 CIA-MI6 coup against Mohammad Mossadegh as the original rupture that pushed Iranian liberals into a lasting despondency captured in Mehdi Akhavan-Sales’s poem 'Winter'. De Bellaigue argues that four and a half decades of theocratic rule, sanctions, and recent US-Israeli strikes have not produced the regime collapse Washington and Tel Aviv expected; instead, the Islamic Republic has turned assassination and hardship into propaganda victories, leaving Iran’s middle class inured to privation and its youth wi [TRUNCATED]

ProPublica investigates the surge in immigration scams under Trump’s mass-deportation campaign. The piece follows Jasmir Urbina, an asylum-seeker who paid nearly $10,000 to a fake ‘Catholic Charities’ attorney via WhatsApp and Zelle, only to discover she had been defrauded. Reports of such notario fraud have doubled nationwide as fear of ICE raids drives migrants to seek urgent—and often fraudulent—legal help. The article documents how scammers exploit Facebook ads, virtual hearings, and official-looking insignia to mimic government processes, leaving victims both poorer and without legitimate [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova distills Oliver Sacks’s posthumous essay collection The River of Consciousness to explore how perception is not passive reception but creative construction. Drawing on neurological case studies of patients whose consciousness ‘freezes’ while the world continues, Sacks argues that motion, color, and depth are qualia—subjective experiences—produced by transient coalitions of neurons that must cross a threshold and recruit frontal-lobe allies to reach awareness. The essay frames perception as a mirror reflecting the observer’s singular history, making intersubjective understanding an [TRUNCATED]

The Taya Necklace offers a privacy-focused alternative at $89, using directional mics and speaker verification to only transcribe the wearer's voice while filtering out bystanders. Humy 2.0 expanded beyond social studies to support cross-curricular learning with historical figures explaining STEM concepts. Monsha and GoTeach provide comprehensive AI lesson planning for social studies with differentiation features and stand [TRUNCATED]

Reflecti AI Memory Pendant offers encrypted, searchable life-logging with daily summaries and end-to-end encryption. The LUCI Pin provides privacy-first life-logging with local AI processing and 4K recording capabilities. Anypin Note Pod P1 delivers professional-grade voice recording with 120+ language transcription and context-aware recording features.

newyorker.title: Ellen Burstyn’s Inner Library
newyorker.dek: Kris Kristofferson told her he was a poet when they co-starred in “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore.” Her new book tells the story of her life in poetry, Michael Schulman writes.
newyorker.text:
The actress Ellen Burstyn has what she calls an “inner library” of poetry that she has memorized in the course of her long life. “I’m ninety-three now, so I probably have less in my head than I used to when it comes to poems,” she said the other day. “But some have been there for years. Decades.” Burstyn was in her Upper West Side apartment, which was festooned with books, plants, crystals, orbs, and statues of Shiva and Buddha and Guanyin (“the goddess of compassion,” she noted). Meditative music piped in through the sound system. “I was getting a massage, so it’s left over,” she explained.
She wore green slacks, a green turtleneck, and a green cardigan (“I do like green”), with chunky metallic necklaces. In her writing room, lined with shelves of Mary Oliver and Rumi—her Oscar, for “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore,” was across the hall—she showed off the desk where she wrote her new book, “Poetry Says It Better,” in which she revisits her life story through verses she has loved. “I like what metaphors set off in your mind,” she said. “I like what poetry plays with.”
She settled into a wicker chair in the living room, beneath a wooden mermaid that she got in Mexico. Her cavapoo, Kerri, sat regally on a divan. The first poem that spoke to Burstyn, she recalled, was William Ernest Henley’s “Invictus,” which she read in high school, in Detroit. “ ‘I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul’—that awakened an understanding in me that life was not always going to be the way it was.” At school, she was head cheerleader, but her home life was arduous. “My mother married a man who hated her two children,” she said. “I was being punished a lot physically.” She wrote her own poem and gave it to a boy in her class. “He said, ‘Just because your name is Edna’—which was my original name—‘doesn’t mean you have to copy her style.’ I said, ‘Who?’ ” That’s how she discovered Edna St. Vincent Millay. “It was like she was my voice,” she said, and rattled off Millay’s sonnet “Love Is Not All.”
At nineteen, Burstyn married her first husband, a poet and car salesman (“In Detroit, everybody’s involved with cars”), who introduced her to Baudelaire. “When I read Baudelaire now, I go, Boy, he’s really dark!” she said. By twenty-four, she was modelling in New York and decided to become an actress. “I went around saying, ‘I’m going to do a Broadway play this fall. Do you know how I can get an audition?’ ” It worked: she made her Broadway début in 1957, in the comedy “Fair Game.” Her determination put her in mind of the poem “For Longing,” by John O’Donohue: “May you have the courage to listen to the voice of desire.”
Some years later, in Hollywood, she was acting in the Debbie Reynolds flick “Goodbye Charlie.” “I said, ‘This is it. This is the big time. Next I’ll be playing Debbie Reynolds’s part!’ And this voice in my head said, I don’t want it,” she recalled. Through Shelley Winters, she met the acting teacher Lee Strasberg and studied with him. “I did a scene for him, and he said, ‘I don’t know, dear. You either laugh or you cry. If you would laugh and cry at the same time, then we’d see something.’ ” She found herself doing just that in her breakout film, “The Last Picture Show.” This reminded her of what the poet Wisława Szymborska wrote about the soul: “Joy and sorrow / aren’t two different feelings for it.”
Burstyn starred in “Alice” in 1974, alongside Kris Kristofferson, who insisted that he couldn’t act or sing. “What is it you do?” she remembered asking him. “I am a poet,” he answered. In her book, she included a sampling of his lyrics. (“He’s a walkin’ contradiction, partly truth and partly fiction.”) Did any poems remind her of starring in “The Exorcist”? Not quite, she said, but the film does open with the Muslim call to prayer. In the seventies, inspired by an etching of a chanting muezzin in her copy of “The Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám,” she began travelling the Middle East. In Iran, she visited the Tomb of Hafez. “People come reading Hafez’s poetry,” she said, recalling a path lined with roses “about at nose height, so as you’re walking you’re sniffing a different rose each step.”
In 1995, she was in “How to Make an American Quilt”; her co-star Maya Angelou would recite poems for her. “The makeup department threw up their hands, because I had to be completely re-made up after lunch every day from crying in Maya’s trailer,” Burstyn recalled. “So then I snuck in and brought Q-tips.” She leaned back in her chair and mimed holding the cotton tips to her eyes. “I would lay down in her trailer with Q-tips and go, ‘O.K., hit me!’ ” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Known World
literary_pick.author: Edward P. Jones
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2004 Pulitzer Prize for Fiction and hailed by the New York Times as the best book of fiction of the 21st century, The Known World is a debut novel of stunning emotional depth and literary power. Set in antebellum Virginia, it tells the story of Henry Townsend, a former slave who becomes proprietor of his own plantation—and his own slaves. When Henry dies, his widow Caldonia succumbs to grief, and the plantation begins to unravel as slaves escape and families betray one another. The novel explores the moral complexities of slavery and freedom, weaving together the lives of the ens [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: 2 former Chinese defence ministers handed suspended death sentences
enriched_articles.text: Two former Chinese defence ministers, Wei Fenghe and Li Shangfu, have been sentenced to death with reprieve on corruption charges, according to Xinhua. A military court found Wei guilty of accepting bribery, while Li was deemed guilty of both accepting and offering bribes. Both men were sentenced to death with a two-year reprieve, deprivation of political rights for life and confiscation of all personal property. After the two-year reprieve period, their sentence would be commuted to life impris

enriched_articles.title: China gives suspended death sentences to 2 ex-defense ministers accused of bribery
enriched_articles.text: A Chinese military court gave suspended death sentences Thursday to two former defense ministers who were accused of bribery, according to the official Xinhua News Agency. The court said Wei Fenghe was guilty of accepting bribes and sentenced to death with a two-year reprieve. It said Li Shangfu was guilty of accepting and offering bribes, and handed him the same sentence. Suspended death sentences are often commuted to life in prison in China.

enriched_articles.title: Iran war updates: Tehran says US violated ceasefire, targeted ships
enriched_articles.text: Iran has accused the United States of violating a ceasefire by targeting two ships at the Strait of Hormuz and attacking civilian areas. The US fired on an Iranian oil tanker in the Gulf of Oman as President Donald Trump increased pressure on Tehran to accept a deal to end the war. Iran said it was reviewing a US peace proposal that sources said would formally end the war, while leaving unresolved Washington's demands that Tehran suspend its nuclear programme and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

enriched_articles.title: Hantavirus being monitored in US. Latest on cruise ship outbreak
enriched_articles.text: Officials in the United States are monitoring Americans who traveled on the cruise ship at the center of the hantavirus outbreak that has caused three deaths. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said in a May 6 news release that the State Department is leading a coordinated response and is in contact with passengers and health authorities around the world. Eight people who traveled on the MV Hondius have been linked to the outbreak so far. Three people have died.

enriched_articles.title: More than 100 people stranded on cruise ship after deadly outbreak
enriched_articles.text: A suspected outbreak of the rare hantavirus infection on a cruise ship in the Atlantic Ocean killed three people, including an elderly married couple, and sickened at least three others, the World Health Organization and South Africa's Department of Health said Sunday. The outbreak happened on the MV Hondius cruise ship, which had left Argentina around three weeks ago for a cruise that included visits to Antarctica, the Falkland Islands and other stops.
